mnemonics: NESTED CIRCLES GET SMALLER, TOUCH A SINGLE POINT: Think of a set of Russian dolls nested inside each other, each one smaller than the last. The theorem says that if you keep shrinking them perfectly (closed and complete space), you'll eventually be left with a single, tiny doll (the unique intersection point).
keyPoints: The theorem requires a sequence of nested, non-empty, closed subsets in a complete metric space.
The diameters of the sets must approach zero.
The conclusion is that the intersection of all these sets contains exactly one point.
commonMistakes: Forgetting the condition that the space must be complete (e.g., applying it to the rational numbers).
Assuming the theorem holds if the diameters do not shrink to zero (the intersection could be empty or contain more than one point).
